name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ ٱلْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُۥ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ رَّحْمَةً وَعِلْمًا فَٱغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ سَبِيلَكَ وَقِهِمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٧)
```

```english
Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it, celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him, and they plead for forgiveness for the faithful: ‘Our Lord! You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge. So forgive those who repent and follow Your way and save them from the punishment of hell. (7)
```

```arabic
رَبَّنَا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ٱلَّتِى وَعَدتَّهُمْ وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَأَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٨)
```

```english
Our Lord! Admit them into the Gardens of Eden, which You have promised them, along with whoever is righteous among their forebears, their spouses and their descendants. Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise. (8)
```

```arabic
وَقِهِمُ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ وَمَن تَقِ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمْتَهُۥ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٩)
```

```english
Save them from the ills [of the Day of Retribution]; and whomever You save from the ills that day, You will have had mercy upon him, and that is the great triumph.’ (9)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُنَادَوْنَ لَمَقْتُ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ مِن مَّقْتِكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ إِذْ تُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَتَكْفُرُونَ (١٠)
```

```english
It will be proclaimed to the faithless: ‘Surely, Allah’s outrage [towards you] is greater than your outrage towards yourselves, as you were invited to faith, but you disbelieved.’ (10)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَمَتَّنَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَٱعْتَرَفْنَا بِذُنُوبِنَا فَهَلْ إِلَىٰ خُرُوجٍ مِّن سَبِيلٍ (١١)
```

```english
They will say, ‘Our Lord! Twice did You make us die, and twice did You give us life. We admit our sins. Is there any way out [from this plight]?’ (11)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكُم بِأَنَّهُۥٓ إِذَا دُعِىَ ٱللَّهُ وَحْدَهُۥ كَفَرْتُمْ ۖ وَإِن يُشْرَكْ بِهِۦ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ ۚ فَٱلْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ ٱلْعَلِىِّ ٱلْكَبِيرِ (١٢)
```

```english
[They will be told,] ‘This [plight of yours] is because, when Allah was invoked alone, you would disbelieve, but if partners were ascribed to Him you would believe. So the judgment belongs to Allah, the All-exalted, the All-great.’ (12)
```

## (Commentary)

When the Glorified mentioned the denial of those who disbelieve and their arguing against the signs of Allah with falsehood, He alluded to the fact that they cannot frustrate Him, nor are they overlooked. Rather, they are intended by this invitation, and the concern regarding them is that they be distinguished so that the word of punishment may be justified against them and they may be penalized. He then returned to the beginning of the discourse, in which He indicated that the revelation of the Book and the establishment of the invitation were for the forgiveness of a group and the acceptance of their repentance, and for the punishment of others. Thus, He mentioned that people, in the face of this invitation, are of two groups: a group for whom the bearers of the Throne and those around it from among the angels seek forgiveness—these are the repentant ones to Allah who follow His path, along with the righteous among their fathers, spouses, and progeny—and a group who are detested and punished, and they are those who disbelieve in monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*.

His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ** — "Those who bear the Throne and those who are around it celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him" (40:7), until the end of the verse. The Glorified did not identify who these bearers of the Throne are, nor is there an explicit statement in His speech that they are from the angels. However, the conjunction of His saying: **وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ** (and those who are around it) with them—while He has said regarding those around it: **وَتَرَى الْمَلائِكَةَ حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ** — "And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne" (39:75)—suggests that the bearers of the Throne are also from the angels.

The detailed discussion regarding the meaning of the Throne (العرش) has already been presented in the eighth volume of this book.

Thus, His saying: **الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ** refers to the angels who bear the Throne from which the commands appear and the divine decrees are issued, by which the world is governed; and those around the Throne are the angels who are the near-stationed (المقربون) among them.

His saying: **يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ** means they declare the transcendence of Allah, the Glorified, while their glorification of Him is accompanied by praise for their Lord. They exalt Him, the Exalted, above everything that does not befit the sanctuary of His holiness—including the existence of a partner in His kingdom—and they praise Him for His actions and His governance.

His saying: **وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ** means their faith in Him—given this state where the Throne of sovereignty and governance belongs to Allah and they are its bearers or those circling around it to receive commands, exalting Him above every deficiency and praising Him for His acts—signifies faith in His oneness in His Lordship (الربوبية) and His Divinity (الألوهية). In mentioning the Throne and attributing glorification, praise, and faith to the angels, there is a refutation of the polytheists, as they consider the near-stationed angels to be partners with Allah in His Lordship and Divinity, taking them as lords and gods whom they worship.

His saying: **وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** means they ask Allah, the Glorified, to forgive those who believe.

His saying: **رَبَّنا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَحْمَةً وَعِلْماً** — "Our Lord, You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge" (40:7), etc., is a narration of the text of their seeking forgiveness. They began it by praising Him, the Exalted, for the vastness of His mercy and knowledge. They mentioned mercy and coupled it with knowledge because, through His mercy, He bestows favors upon every needy one—mercy being the principle of the emanation of every blessing—and through His knowledge, He knows the need of every needy one who is prepared for mercy.

His saying: **فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ وَقِهِمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ** — "So forgive those who repent and follow Your way, and save them from the punishment of hell" (40:7), is a derivation from the praise they offered regarding the vastness of His mercy and knowledge. What is meant by the "way" (السبيل) they followed is what was legislated for them of the religion (الدين), which is Islam (الإسلام), and their following it is the application of their deeds to it. Thus, what is meant by their repentance (توبتهم) is their return to Him, the Exalted, through faith. The meaning is: forgive those who returned to You by believing in Your oneness and traversing Your path, which is Islam, and protect them from the punishment of hell, which is the ultimate goal and purpose of forgiveness.

His saying, the Exalted: **رَبَّنا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدْتَهُمْ** — "Our Lord, admit them into the Gardens of Eden, which You have promised them" (40:8), until the end of the verse. The repetition of the call with the word "Our Lord" (ربنا) is for increased entreaty. What is meant by the "promise" is His promise to them through the tongues of His messengers and in His books.

His saying: **وَمَنْ صَلَحَ مِنْ آبائِهِمْ وَأَزْواجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ** — "along with whoever is righteous among their forefathers, their spouses and their descendants" (40:8), is conjoined to the position of the pronoun in His saying: **وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ** (and admit them). What is meant by "righteousness" (الصلوح) is the suitability for entering Paradise. The meaning is: and admit those who are suitable for entering Paradise from among their fathers, spouses, and progeny into the Gardens of Eden.

Furthermore, it is known from the context of the verses that their seeking of forgiveness is for the general body of believers. It is also known that they divided them into two groups: they divided them into those who repented and followed the way of Allah, whom Allah promised the Gardens of Eden, and those who were righteous. They made the first group the followed and the second group the followers.

It appears from this that the first group are those who are complete in faith and action, as necessitated by the reality of the meaning of their saying: **لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ** (for those who repent and follow Your way). Thus, they mentioned them and asked Him to forgive them and fulfill for them what He promised them of the Gardens of Eden. The second group is below these in rank, consisting of those who did not complete their faith and action—those with deficient faith, the oppressed (المستضعف), and those with bad deeds among the relatives of the first group. So they mentioned them and asked Him, the Exalted, to join them with the first group of complete believers in their gardens and to protect them from evil deeds.

Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — "As for those who have faith and whose descendants follow them in faith, We will unite their descendants with them, and We will not stint them anything of their deeds" (52:21). However, the verse we are currently discussing is broader and more inclusive, as it includes fathers and spouses, unlike the verse in Surah al-Tur. Moreover, what is stipulated here is "righteousness" (الصلوح), which is more general than the "faith" (الإيمان) stipulated in the verse of al-Tur.

His saying: **إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise" (40:8), is a justification for their saying: **فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا** (so forgive those who repent), until the end of their request. What the apparent context would seem to require is to say: "Indeed You are the All-Forgiving, the All-Merciful." However, it shifted to mentioning the two attributes: the All-Mighty (العزيز) and the All-Wise (الحكيم), because at the opening of their request, there occurred the praise of Him, the Exalted, with their saying: **رَبَّنا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَحْمَةً وَعِلْماً**. The necessity of the vastness of mercy—which is the generality of bestowing—is that He has the right to give what He wills to whom He wills and withhold what He wills from whom He wills; and this is the meaning of might (العزة), which is the power to bestow and withhold. The necessity of the vastness of knowledge of everything is that knowledge permeates all aspects of the action, such that ignorance does not enter any part of it; and its necessity is the perfection of the action, which is wisdom (الحكمة).

Thus, His saying: **إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** is in the sense of seeking intercession (الاستشفاع) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — through the vastness of His mercy and the vastness of His knowledge, both mentioned at the opening of the request as a preparation and preamble for mentioning the need, which is forgiveness and Paradise.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقِهِمُ السَّيِّئاتِ وَمَنْ تَقِ السَّيِّئاتِ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمْتَهُ** — "And save them from the ills; and whomever You save from the ills on that day, You will have had mercy upon him" (40:9), etc. The apparent context is that the pronoun in **قِهِمُ** (save them) refers to both those who repented and those who were righteous.

What is meant by "ills" (السيئات)—according to what has been said—are the consequences of sins, which are their punishment. The consequences are called "ills" because the recompense for an ill is an ill. The Exalted said: **وَجَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِثْلُها** — "The recompense of an ill is an ill like it" (42:40).

It has also been said: what is meant by "ills" are the acts of disobedience and sins themselves, and the speech assumes a suppressed word, the estimation being: "save them from the *recompense* of ills" or "the *punishment* of ills."

The apparent meaning is that the verse is among those indicating that the recompense is the deeds themselves, their good and their evil. Similar statements have been repeated in His speech, such as His saying: **إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "You are only being requited for what you used to do" (66:7).

In any case, the "ills" from which they asked for protection are the horrors and hardships they will face on the Day of Resurrection, other than the punishment of hell. Thus, there is no repetition in their two sayings: **وَقِهْمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ** (and save them from the punishment of hell) and **وَقِهِمُ السَّيِّئاتِ** (and save them from the ills).

It has also been said: what is meant by "ills" are the sins themselves in this world, and their saying: **يَوْمَئِذٍ** (on that day) refers to the world. The meaning would be: "and protect them from committing sins and engaging in them in the world through Your success-granting (توفيق)."

However, the context supports that what is meant by "on that day" is the Day of Resurrection, as evidenced by their saying: **وَقِهِمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ** and their saying: **وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ**, etc. Thus, the truth is that what is meant by "ills" are the horrors and hardships that will appear to people on the Day of Resurrection.

From these verses containing the prayer and request of the angels, it appears:

First: That part of the etiquette of prayer (الدعاء) is to begin with His praise and glorification, then to mention the need, and then to seek intercession through His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) that are appropriate to it.

Second: That the request for forgiveness precedes the request for Paradise. The mention of forgiveness before Paradise has occurred frequently in His speech when they are mentioned together. This is in accordance with rational consideration, for the attainment of readiness for any blessing occurs by the removal of the obstacle before the attainment of the blessing itself.

Some have mentioned that in His saying: **فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا**, the verse indicates that the dropping of punishment upon repentance is a favor (تفضل) from Allah, the Exalted. For if it were obligatory, there would be no need for their request; rather, Allah, the Glorified, would inevitably do it.

However, the fact that the issuance of an act from Him, the Exalted, is necessary does not negate the validity of requesting and seeking it from Him, as evidenced by their saying after seeking forgiveness: **رَبَّنا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدْتَهُمْ**. They asked for Paradise for them while acknowledging that Allah had promised it to them, and His promise is obligatory to fulfill, for He does not break His promise. More explicit than this verse is His saying narrating from the believers: **رَبَّنا وَآتِنا ما وَعَدْتَنا عَلى رُسُلِكَ وَلا تُخْزِنا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّكَ لا تُخْلِفُ الْمِيعادَ** — "Our Lord, give us what You have promised us through Your apostles, and do not disgrace us on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed You do not break Your promise" (3:194).

The acceptance of repentance is among the things Allah has made obligatory upon Himself and has made a right for the repentant ones. The Exalted said: **إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ فَأُولئِكَ يَتُوبُ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ** — "Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only for those who commit evil out of ignorance and then repent promptly. It is such whose repentance Allah will accept" (4:17). Thus, seeking every right that Allah has made obligatory upon Himself—such as asking for forgiveness for the repentant—is in reality a return to Him to seek the fulfillment of what He promised and an expression of longing to attain His honor.

Likewise, His favor (تفضل) does not necessitate that the act be merely permissible to issue and not obligatory. Every gift of His gifts is a favor, whether its issuance was obligatory or not. For if an act among His acts were obligatory to issue from Him, its obligation upon Him would not be by the influence of another upon Him or by coercion, as He is the influencer in everything and nothing else influences Him. Rather, that would be by an obligation from Him, the Exalted, upon Himself. Its meaning boils down to His decree (قضاء) to perform an act and to emanate a gift as a decisive decree. Thus, the Glorified only performs it by a volition from Himself, transcendent above being compelled by another, doing so as a favor. So the act is a favor from Him even if its issuance is obligatory. As for if the act were not obligatory to issue, its being a favor is even clearer.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يُنادَوْنَ لَمَقْتُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ مَقْتِكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِذْ تُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى الْإِيمانِ فَتَكْفُرُونَ** — "Indeed those who disbelieve will be told, ‘Allah’s abhorrence [of you] is greater than your abhorrence of yourselves, for you would disbelieve when you were summoned to faith’" (40:10). **Abhorrence** (المقت) is the most intense hatred. When He mentioned the believers with some of what they have by virtue of their faith, He returned to mentioning the disbelievers with some of what is against them by virtue of their disbelief.

The apparent meaning of the verse and the following verse is that this mentioned call is addressed to them in the Hereafter after entering the Fire, when they taste the punishment for their disbelief. It then becomes clear to them that their disbelief in the world—when they were being called by the prophets to faith—was an abhorrence and intense hatred from them toward themselves, as they brought themselves thereby to the place of permanent destruction.

They are called from the side of Allah, the Glorified, and it is said to them: "I swear, Allah's abhorrence and intense hatred for you is greater than your abhorrence of yourselves and your intense hatred for them when you were being called"—narrating a past state—"to faith by the prophets, yet you disbelieved."

His saying, the Exalted: **قالُوا رَبَّنا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ فَاعْتَرَفْنا بِذُنُوبِنا فَهَلْ إِلى خُرُوجٍ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ** — "They will say, ‘Our Lord! Twice did You make us die, and twice did You give us life. We admit our sins. Is there any way out?’" (40:11). The context of the verse and what precedes it suggests that they say this after hearing the previous call. They only say it while they are in the Fire, as evidenced by their saying: **فَهَلْ إِلى خُرُوجٍ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ** (Is there any way out?).

Presenting this confession from them is a kind of seeking a cause and a means to escape the punishment, but there is no time for escape. This is because, while they were in the world, they were in doubt regarding the Resurrection and the return to Allah, so they denied it and forgot the Day of Reckoning. Forgetting that was the cause of their indulgence in sins and their headlong rush into acts of disobedience. Forgetting the Day of Reckoning is the key to every disobedience and misguidance. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ** — "Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because they forgot the Day of Reckoning" (38:26).

Then, when Allah caused them to die death after death and gave them life life after life, their doubt regarding the matter of the Resurrection and the return to Allah vanished due to what they witnessed of survival after death and life after life. They used to think that death was annihilation, saying: "It is nothing but our life in this world, and we shall not be resurrected."

In short, doubt vanished from them with the attainment of certainty (اليقين), but the sins and acts of disobedience remained. Therefore, they sought a means to escape the punishment through confession. Sometimes they confessed to the attainment of certainty, as Allah narrated from them in His saying: **وَلَوْ تَرى إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ** — "Were you to see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [saying], ‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard. Send us back so that we may act righteously. Indeed we are now certain’" (32:12). At other times, they confessed to their sins, as in the verse under discussion. They used to think they were free and independent in their will and actions, entitled to wish what they wished and do what they did, with no reckoning and no sin.

From this, the reason for the sequence of their saying: **فَاعْتَرَفْنا بِذُنُوبِنا** (so we admit our sins) following their saying: **أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ** (Twice did You make us die, and twice did You give us life) becomes clear. The confession is, in reality, sequenced upon the attainment of certainty regarding the Return (المعاد), which necessitates the knowledge that their deviations from the way of Allah were misguidance and sins.

What is meant by their saying: **أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ**—as has been said—is the causing of death from the life of this world and the giving of life for the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection*, then the causing of death from the intermediate realm and the giving of life for the Reckoning on the Day of Resurrection. Thus, the verse refers to the death after the life of this world and the death after the life of the intermediate realm, and to the life in the intermediate realm and the life for the Day of Resurrection. Were it not for the life of the intermediate realm, the second death would not be realized, because both death and life depend for their realization on the precedence of their opposite.

They did not mention the life of this world, nor did they say: "and You gave us life thrice," even though it was a giving of life because it occurred after the death which is the state of the non-existence of the spirit's entry. This is because their intention was to mention the giving of life which is the cause of certainty in the Return, which is the life in the intermediate realm and then in the Resurrection. As for the worldly life, although it was a giving of life, it did not by itself necessitate certainty in the Return, for they were in doubt regarding the Return while they were alive in the world.

From the preceding explanation, the invalidity of the objection becomes clear—the objection that if the two lives meant those in the intermediate realm and the Hereafter, it would have been necessary to say: "You made us die twice and gave us life thrice," since the intention is only to mention the deaths and lives they passed through, which are two deaths and three lives.

The answer is that the intention is not merely to mention the deaths and lives they passed through in whatever manner they were, but rather to mention those among them that bequeathed certainty in the Return, and the worldly life is not of this description.

It has also been said: what is meant by the first death is the state of the sperm before the entry of the spirit, and by the first life what is the state of the human after its entry; by the second death his death in the world, and by the second life his being given life by the Resurrection for the Reckoning on the Day of Resurrection. The verse would then correspond to what is in His saying, the Exalted: **كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِاللهِ وَكُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ** — "How can you deny Allah? You were lifeless and He gave you life, then He will make you die, then He will bring you to life" (2:28).

However, when they sensed that "death" (الإماتة) does not truly apply to the state of the human before the entry of the spirit into his body—because it depends on the precedence of life—they resorted to strange interpretations to justify it. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to *al-Kashshaf* and its commentaries.

Furthermore, you have already known that their mention of the deaths and lives they passed through is an allusion to the causes of their attaining certainty in the Return, and the worldly life and the death preceding it have no effect on that.

It has also been said: the first life is in the world and the second in the grave; the first death is in the world and the second in the grave. There is no mention in the verse of the life of the Day of Resurrection. This is refuted by what was mentioned before: that the worldly life has no connection to the purpose, so there is no reason to mention it, whereas the life on the Day of Resurrection is the opposite of that.

It has also been said: what is meant by the two lives is the life of the Resurrection and the life preceding it. The life of the Resurrection is of two types: life in the grave and life at the Resurrection. This division was not addressed in the verse, so the verse would include all three lives and both deaths.

This is refuted by what refutes the two previous views, in addition to the point that the mention of the second death which is in the grave is evidence that the division is intended, and what is meant is numerical plurality (التعدد الشخصي), not generic (النوعي).

It has also been said: what is meant is the giving of life to the souls in the world of particles (عالم الذر), then death, then life in the world, then death, then life for the Resurrection. This is refuted by what refutes its predecessors.

It has also been said: what is meant by the dual form is repetition, as in His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ** — "Then look again" (67:4). The meaning would be: "You made us die death after death and gave us life life after life."

This was objected to on the grounds that it would only be valid if the statement were: "You made us die two deaths and gave us life two lives" or "two times," for example. But what was said is the number itself, and it does not admit that possibility, as has been said regarding His saying: **إِلهَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ** — "two gods" (16:51).

Their saying: **فَهَلْ إِلى خُرُوجٍ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ** is a prayer and a request in the form of an interrogation. In the indefiniteness of "exit" (خروج) and "way" (سبيل), there is an indication of their satisfaction with any kind of exit through any possible way. For exhaustion has reached its peak with them, and the day is a day when all means have been cut off from them, so there is no means whose effect can be hoped for in their escape from the punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكُمْ بِأَنَّهُ إِذا دُعِيَ اللهُ وَحْدَهُ كَفَرْتُمْ وَإِنْ يُشْرَكْ بِهِ تُؤْمِنُوا** — "This is because when Allah was invoked alone, you would disbelieve, but if partners were ascribed to Him, you would believe" (40:12), etc. This is a discourse of severity toward the disbelievers, its place being the Day of Resurrection. It is possible that its place is the world, addressed to them for the purpose of deterring them from polytheism (الشرك).

The indication in His saying: **ذلِكُمْ** (This) is to the hardship they are in. In His saying: **وَإِنْ يُشْرَكْ بِهِ** (but if partners were ascribed to Him), there is an indication of persistence. The speech is intended to explain their stubbornness against the truth and their enmity toward His oneness. They disbelieve in everything in which a trace of monotheism appears, and they believe in everything that bears the mark of polytheism. They do not observe any right for Allah, nor do they respect any side of His. Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, forbids them His mercy and does not observe any side for them in His judgment.

In this sense, His saying: **فَالْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ الْعَلِيِّ الْكَبِيرِ** — "So the judgment belongs to Allah, the All-exalted, the All-great" (40:12), connects to the beginning of the verse and is derived from it. It is as if it were said: "Since you have cut yourselves off from Allah entirely, and disbelieved in everything He desires, and believed in everything He dislikes, He cuts Himself off from you and judges concerning you as He judges, without any consideration for your state."

Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying: **نَسُوا اللهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ** — "They forgot Allah, so He forgot them" (9:67). The sentence—I mean His saying: **فَالْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ الْعَلِيِّ الْكَبِيرِ**—is specific according to the context, even if it is general in itself. In it is a threat, and the threat is emphasized by its conclusion with the two names: the All-Exalted (العلي) and the All-Great (الكبير).
